event: Icon of the Virgin Mary Hagiosoritissa in prayer
symbols: hands raised in prayer/intercession — Mary's role as intercessor before God
dark blue maphorion (veil) — heavenly royalty and humility
golden halo — sanctity and the glory of the Theotokos
Greek inscriptions — sacred identification in the language of the early Church
half-length frontal view — direct spiritual encounter and accessibility to the worshiper
small medallion saints at bottom — the communion of saints surrounding the Mother of God
description: A Byzantine icon on a worn wooden panel depicting the Virgin Mary in half-length view. She wears a dark blue maphorion (veil and mantle) over a darker inner garment, with a golden halo behind her head. Her face is rendered in the stylized Byzantine manner with large dark eyes, a long nose, and a small mouth. Greek inscriptions appear on either side of her figure and around the margins. Small circular medallions containing busts of saints line the bottom edge of the panel. The icon shows significant age with paint loss, cracking, and wear, particularly around the edges.
theological_description: The Hagiosoritissa type icon depicts the Virgin Mary in a posture of intercession, her hands raised in prayer — a visual representation of her perpetual advocacy before God on behalf of humanity. This icon type takes its name from the Holy Soros (Hagia Soros), the chapel in Constantinople that housed the Virgin's belt relic. The theological foundation of Marian intercession rests on Mary's unique role at the Incarnation: she who said 'Let it be to me according to your word' (Luke 1:38) continues to intercede for believers. At the wedding in Cana, Mary's intercession — 'They have no wine' (John 2:3) — prompted Christ's first miracle, establishing a biblical pattern of her mediating role. The Byzantine tradition of the Theotokos (God-bearer) affirms that Mary's unique relationship with Christ gives her prayers a particular efficacy. The communion of saints depicted in the medallions below reinforces the theology of the Church as a praying body spanning heaven and earth, united in Christ. The icon's worn, ancient surface testifies to centuries of veneration and prayer before this image.

scripture_refs.reason: Mary's fiat — the foundational act of faith that grounds her intercessory role
Mary's intercession at Cana — the biblical model for her mediating prayer
All generations will call me blessed — fulfilled in the centuries of veneration this icon represents
Mary praying with the apostles — her continuing role in the prayer life of the Church
Supplications and intercessions for all people — the theology of intercessory prayer Mary embodies
